tagline: Estimate legal fees by case type and hourly rate
keywords: attorney fee calculator, lawyer cost estimator, legal fees, hourly rate lawyer
calcFn: var total = hourlyRate * estHours; var after = Math.max(0, total - retainer); document.getElementById('totalFees').textContent = dollar(total); document.getElementById('afterRetainer').textContent = dollar(after); document.getElementById('perMonth').textContent = dollar(total / 6) + '/month';
formulas: Total Fees = Hourly Rate × Estimated Hours
Retainer is an upfront deposit deducted from total
Average attorney rates: $200-500/hr depending on specialty and market

faq.question: How much does a lawyer cost per hour?
faq.answer: Average: $200-400/hr. Small town: $150-250. Major city: $300-700+. Specialty matters: family law $200-350, corporate $400-700, IP $350-550. Many offer free initial consultations.

faq.question: What is a retainer fee?
faq.answer: A retainer is an upfront deposit held in a trust account. The lawyer bills against it hourly. Once depleted, you'll be billed directly. Typical retainers range from $2,000-10,000 depending on case complexity.

faq.question: Are there alternatives to hourly billing?
faq.answer: Yes: flat fee (common for simple matters), contingency fee (33-40% of settlement — common in personal injury), and hybrid arrangements. Always discuss fee structure before hiring.

tagline: Estimate court filing costs by case type
keywords: court filing fee, legal filing cost, lawsuit cost, court fees
calcFn: var fees = {small: 75, civil: 350, divorce: 300, bankruptcy: 338, federal: 405}; var filing = fees[caseType] || 350; if (caseType === 'small' && amount > 10000) filing = 100; if (caseType === 'civil' && amount > 100000) filing = 500; var service = caseType === 'small' ? 40 : 85; document.getElementById('filingFee').textContent = dollar(filing); document.getElementById('serviceFee').textContent = dollar(service); document.getElementById('totalInitial').textContent = dollar(filing + service);
formulas: Filing fees vary by court type, jurisdiction, and amount in dispute
Small claims: $30-100, Civil: $200-500, Federal: $405
Service of process: $40-100 per defendant

faq.question: How much does it cost to file a lawsuit?
faq.answer: Small claims: $30-100. State civil court: $200-500. Federal court: $405. Divorce: $200-400. Bankruptcy: $338 (Chapter 7) or $313 (Chapter 13). These are just filing fees — attorney fees are separate.

faq.question: Can filing fees be waived?
faq.answer: Yes, courts offer fee waivers (in forma pauperis) for people who can't afford them. You'll need to submit an application showing income below a threshold — typically 125-200% of the federal poverty level.

faq.question: What other court costs should I expect?
faq.answer: Beyond filing: service of process ($40-100), deposition transcripts ($200-1,000+), expert witnesses ($2,000-10,000+), mediation ($500-2,000), and appeal fees ($500-750). Litigation costs add up quickly beyond the initial filing.

keywords: foia response time, foia calculator, freedom of information request, foia processing
calcFn: var times = {federal:{simple:20,complex:90,expedited:10},state:{simple:10,complex:30,expedited:5},local:{simple:7,complex:21,expedited:3}}; var t = (times[agency] || times.federal)[complexity] || 20; var legal = agency === 'federal' ? 20 : agency === 'state' ? '10-15' : '5-10'; document.getElementById('estDays').textContent = t + ' business days (typical)'; document.getElementById('deadline').textContent = legal + ' business days (statutory)'; document.getElementById('appealWindow').textContent = agency === 'federal' ? '90 days to appeal' : 'Varies by jurisdiction';
formulas: Federal FOIA statutory deadline: 20 business days
Complex requests often take 2-6 months in practice
Expedited processing available for urgent need or threat to life

faq.question: How long does a FOIA request take?
faq.answer: The legal deadline is 20 business days for federal agencies, but actual processing often takes 2-6 months for complex requests. Some agencies have backlogs exceeding a year. Simple requests are faster.

faq.question: How do I file a FOIA request?
faq.answer: Federal: submit to the agency's FOIA office (most accept online). Be specific about what you want. Include date ranges, names, and topics. Vague requests take longer. Each agency has its own portal and process.

faq.question: Can a FOIA request be denied?
faq.answer: Yes, under 9 exemptions including national security, personal privacy, trade secrets, and law enforcement. Agencies must specify which exemption applies. You can appeal denials administratively and then sue in federal court.

tagline: Estimate business license and permit fees
keywords: business license cost, business permit fee, LLC cost, business registration fee
calcFn: var stateFees = {sole:{low:25,mid:50,high:100},llc:{low:50,mid:150,high:500},corp:{low:75,mid:200,high:800},nonprofit:{low:50,mid:150,high:400}}; var sf = (stateFees[entityType] || stateFees.llc)[state] || 150; var local = 50; var ein = needsEin === 'yes' ? 0 : 0; var ra = entityType === 'llc' || entityType === 'corp' ? 125 : 0; var total = sf + local + ein + ra; document.getElementById('stateFee').textContent = dollar(sf); document.getElementById('localLicense').textContent = dollar(local) + ' (typical)'; document.getElementById('totalStartup').textContent = dollar(total) + (ra > 0 ? ' (includes $125 registered agent)' : '');
formulas: State filing: $50-800 depending on entity type and state
EIN: Free from IRS (beware paid services)
Registered agent: $50-300/year for LLCs and Corps
Local business license: $25-100 in most jurisdictions

faq.question: How much does an LLC cost?
faq.answer: State filing fees range from $50 (Kentucky) to $500+ (Massachusetts, California). Annual reports add $0-800/year. California charges an extra $800/year minimum franchise tax. Most states: $100-200 to form.

faq.question: Do I need a business license?
faq.answer: Almost always yes. Most cities and counties require a general business license ($25-100/year). Specific industries (food service, contractors, healthcare) need additional permits. Operating without a license risks fines.

faq.question: What is a registered agent?
faq.answer: LLCs and corporations must designate a registered agent to receive legal documents. You can be your own agent (free) or hire a service ($50-300/year). The agent must have a physical address in the state of formation.

keywords: trademark cost, trademark registration fee, trademark filing cost, USPTO trademark
calcFn: var perClass = filingType === 'teas_plus' ? 250 : 350; var gov = perClass * classes; var legal = useAttorney === 'yes' ? 1500 : 0; var total = gov + legal; document.getElementById('govFees').textContent = dollar(gov); document.getElementById('legalFees').textContent = useAttorney === 'yes' ? dollar(legal) + ' (est.)' : 'N/A — self-filing'; document.getElementById('totalCost').textContent = dollar(total); document.getElementById('timeline').textContent = '8-12 months (if no opposition)';
formulas: TEAS Plus: $250/class, TEAS Standard: $350/class
Each trademark class covers one category of goods/services
Attorney fees: $1,000-2,000 for search + filing
Registration valid 10 years, renewable indefinitely

faq.question: How much does a trademark cost?
faq.answer: USPTO filing: $250-350 per class. Attorney: $1,000-2,000 for search and filing. Total for one class: $1,250-2,350. Additional classes add $250-350 each. Maintenance filings due at years 5 and 10.

faq.question: Should I hire a trademark attorney?
faq.answer: Highly recommended. Attorneys conduct thorough searches to avoid conflicts, select the strongest filing basis, and handle office actions. The rejection rate for self-filed applications is significantly higher.

faq.question: How long does trademark registration take?
faq.answer: 8-12 months if straightforward. After filing, USPTO reviews in 3-4 months. If approved, it's published for 30-day opposition period. If no one opposes, registration issues 2-3 months later. Complications can extend to 18+ months.

tagline: Estimate patent filing and prosecution costs
keywords: patent cost calculator, patent filing fee, patent attorney cost, USPTO patent fee
calcFn: var fees = {provisional:{micro:80,small:160,large:320},utility:{micro:430,small:860,large:1720},design:{micro:210,small:420,large:840},plant:{micro:210,small:420,large:840}}; var atty = {provisional:2500,utility:12000,design:3500,plant:5000}; var f = (fees[patentType] || fees.utility)[entitySize] || 860; var a = atty[patentType] || 12000; document.getElementById('filingFees').textContent = dollar(f); document.getElementById('attorneyEst').textContent = dollar(a); document.getElementById('totalEst').textContent = dollar(f + a); var tl = {provisional:'12 months (then must file non-provisional)',utility:'2-3 years',design:'12-18 months',plant:'18-24 months'}; document.getElementById('timeline').textContent = tl[patentType] || '2-3 years';
formulas: Micro entity: 75% fee reduction (income < $229K, ≤4 prior patents)
Small entity: 50% fee reduction (<500 employees)
Utility patent total cost: $10,000-15,000+ through prosecution
Maintenance fees due at years 3.5, 7.5, and 11.5

faq.question: How much does a patent cost total?
faq.answer: Provisional: $2,000-4,000. Utility patent: $10,000-20,000+ through prosecution. Design patent: $3,000-5,000. These include attorney fees, which are the bulk of the cost. DIY filing is risky for utility patents.

faq.question: Should I file a provisional patent first?
faq.answer: Often yes. A provisional costs $2,000-4,000, establishes your filing date, and gives you 12 months to test the market before committing $10,000+ to a full utility patent. You can use 'Patent Pending' status immediately.

faq.question: How long does a patent last?
faq.answer: Utility patents: 20 years from filing. Design patents: 15 years from grant. You must pay maintenance fees at 3.5, 7.5, and 11.5 years to keep utility patents alive. Missing a maintenance fee can kill your patent.

tagline: Calculate how much rent you can afford
keywords: rent affordability calculator, how much rent can I afford, rent budget, 30 percent rule rent
calcFn: var pct = parseFloat(rule) / 100; var max = monthlyIncome * pct; var remaining = monthlyIncome - max - monthlyDebt; var dti = ((max + monthlyDebt) / monthlyIncome) * 100; document.getElementById('maxRent').textContent = dollar(max); document.getElementById('afterRent').textContent = dollar(remaining) + ' for other expenses'; document.getElementById('debtRatio').textContent = pct2 = fmt(dti, 0) + '% DTI';
formulas: 30% Rule: Max rent = Gross monthly income × 0.30
Conservative: 25% leaves more room for savings
Include utilities in your rent budget calculation

faq.question: How much rent can I afford?
faq.answer: The standard guideline is spending no more than 30% of gross monthly income on rent. On a $5,000/month salary, that's $1,500. If you have significant debt or savings goals, aim for 25%.

faq.question: Is the 30% rule still relevant?
faq.answer: It's a useful starting point but depends on your situation. In expensive cities, many people spend 35-40% on rent. If you have no debt and don't own a car, spending more on rent may be fine. If you have student loans, aim lower.

faq.question: Should rent include utilities?
faq.answer: Yes, your total housing cost should stay within the 30% guideline. Add estimated utilities ($100-200/month), renter's insurance ($15-30), and parking if applicable. The true cost of renting is more than just the lease amount.

tagline: Estimate the cost of your move by distance and size
keywords: moving cost calculator, moving estimate, cost to move, relocation cost
calcFn: var base = {studio:{local:{diy:200,partial:400,full:800},long:{diy:600,partial:1500,full:2500},cross:{diy:1200,partial:2500,full:4000}},'2br':{local:{diy:400,partial:800,full:1500},long:{diy:1000,partial:2500,full:4500},cross:{diy:2000,partial:4000,full:7000}},'4br':{local:{diy:600,partial:1200,full:2500},long:{diy:1500,partial:3500,full:7500},cross:{diy:3000,partial:6000,full:12000}}}; var b = ((base[homeSize]||base['2br'])[moveType]||{})[diy]||2000; document.getElementById('estCost').textContent = dollar(b); document.getElementById('lowRange').textContent = dollar(b * 0.75); document.getElementById('highRange').textContent = dollar(b * 1.35);
formulas: Local moves: typically $400-2,500 (by the hour)
Long distance: $2,000-7,500 (by weight and distance)
Cross country: $4,000-12,000+ for full service
DIY saves 50-70% but requires more time and effort

faq.question: How much does it cost to move?
faq.answer: Local: $400-2,500. Long distance (state to state): $2,000-7,500. Cross country: $4,000-12,000+. DIY truck rental: 50-70% less. Costs vary by home size, distance, time of year, and services needed.

faq.question: When is the cheapest time to move?
faq.answer: September through April (off-season) is 20-30% cheaper than peak season (May-August). Mid-month and mid-week moves are cheaper than month-end and weekends. Avoid holidays and the first/last days of the month.

faq.question: What hidden moving costs should I budget for?
faq.answer: Common extras: packing materials ($100-300), insurance/valuation ($50-200), long carry fees ($75-150), stair fees ($75/flight), storage ($100-300/month), tip for movers (15-20%), and cleaning old/new home ($100-300).

tagline: Calculate return on investment for home improvements
keywords: renovation roi calculator, home improvement roi, remodel value, renovation cost vs value
calcFn: var rois = {kitchen:0.72, bathroom:0.64, deck:0.68, roof:0.61, garage:0.94, siding:0.68, window:0.67}; var roi = rois[projectType] || 0.70; var valueAdd = projectCost * roi; var net = projectCost - valueAdd; document.getElementById('estROI').textContent = pct(roi * 100, 0); document.getElementById('valueAdded').textContent = dollar(valueAdd); document.getElementById('netCost').textContent = dollar(net) + ' net cost';
formulas: ROI = Value Added ÷ Project Cost
Garage door: ~94% ROI (best), Kitchen: ~72%, Bathroom: ~64%
ROI based on 2024 Cost vs. Value Report national averages

faq.question: What home renovations have the best ROI?
faq.answer: Top ROI projects: garage door replacement (94%), manufactured stone veneer (91%), minor kitchen remodel (72%), siding (68%), deck addition (68%). Major kitchen and bathroom remodels have lower ROI percentages but add the most absolute value.

faq.question: Is a kitchen remodel worth it?
faq.answer: A mid-range kitchen remodel ($25K-30K) returns about 72% ($18K-22K in value). A major upscale remodel ($80K+) returns only 55-60%. The key: remodel to match your neighborhood, not exceed it.

faq.question: Which renovations should I avoid for ROI?
faq.answer: Swimming pools (return 50% or less), home offices (40-50%), luxury master suites (50-55%), and sunrooms (47%) have the lowest ROI. Do these for enjoyment, not investment. Over-improving for your neighborhood always hurts ROI.

keywords: closing cost calculator, home closing costs, buyer closing costs, closing fee estimator
calcFn: var pcts = {low: 0.025, avg: 0.035, high: 0.05}; var closePct = pcts[state] || 0.035; var loanAmt = homePrice * (1 - downPct/100); var closing = loanAmt * closePct; var downPayment = homePrice * (downPct / 100); document.getElementById('totalClosing').textContent = dollar(closing); document.getElementById('cashNeeded').textContent = dollar(closing + downPayment); document.getElementById('breakdown').textContent = 'Loan origination: ' + dollar(loanAmt * 0.01) + ' | Title: ' + dollar(1500) + ' | Escrow: ' + dollar(homePrice * 0.005);
formulas: Closing costs typically 2-6% of loan amount
Includes: origination, appraisal, title insurance, escrow, recording fees
Buyer pays most fees; seller typically pays agent commissions

faq.question: How much are closing costs?
faq.answer: Average closing costs are 3-4% of the loan amount. On a $350K home with 20% down ($280K loan), expect $8,400-11,200. This includes lender fees, title insurance, appraisal, escrow, and government recording fees.

faq.question: Can I negotiate closing costs?
faq.answer: Yes. You can negotiate lender fees, shop for title insurance (savings of $500-1,000), ask the seller for a credit (common in buyer's markets), and compare loan estimates from multiple lenders. Always negotiate.

faq.question: What is included in closing costs?
faq.answer: Typical breakdown: loan origination (0.5-1%), appraisal ($400-600), title insurance ($1,000-2,000), escrow/attorney ($500-1,500), recording fees ($100-300), home inspection ($300-500), and prepaid taxes/insurance.

tagline: Calculate how much house you can afford
keywords: house affordability calculator, how much house can I afford, home buying budget, mortgage affordability
calcFn: var monthlyIncome = annualIncome / 12; var maxPITI = monthlyIncome * 0.28; var maxTotal = monthlyIncome * 0.36 - monthlyDebt; var maxPayment = Math.min(maxPITI, maxTotal); var piOnly = maxPayment * 0.75; var r = rate / 100 / 12; var n = 360; var maxLoan = piOnly * (Math.pow(1+r,n)-1) / (r * Math.pow(1+r,n)); var maxPrice = maxLoan + downPayment; var dtiPct = ((maxPayment + monthlyDebt) / monthlyIncome) * 100; document.getElementById('maxHome').textContent = dollar(maxPrice); document.getElementById('monthlyPayment').textContent = dollar(maxPayment) + ' (est. PITI)'; document.getElementById('dti').textContent = pct(dtiPct);
formulas: Front-end ratio: housing costs ≤ 28% of gross income
Back-end ratio: total debt ≤ 36% of gross income
PITI = Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance
Max loan calculated from affordable monthly P&I at given rate

faq.question: How much house can I afford on my salary?
faq.answer: General rule: 2.5-3× your annual income. On $85K salary, that's $212K-255K. With a large down payment, you can go higher. The real limit is your monthly payment relative to income (28% rule).

faq.question: What is the 28/36 rule?
faq.answer: Lenders want: no more than 28% of gross income on housing (front-end DTI) and no more than 36% on all debt combined (back-end DTI). Some loan programs allow up to 43-50% back-end DTI.

faq.question: How much should I put down?
faq.answer: 20% avoids PMI ($100-300/month savings). 10% is a strong position. 3-5% is the minimum for conventional loans. FHA allows 3.5%. VA and USDA allow 0% down for eligible borrowers.

tagline: Calculate rental property return on investment
keywords: landlord roi calculator, rental property roi, rental income calculator, investment property returns
calcFn: var cashFlow = monthlyRent - monthlyExpenses - mortgagePayment; var annual = cashFlow * 12; var cashReturn = (annual / downPay) * 100; var noi = (monthlyRent - monthlyExpenses) * 12; var capRate2 = (noi / purchasePrice) * 100; document.getElementById('monthlyCashFlow').textContent = dollar(cashFlow); document.getElementById('annualCashFlow').textContent = dollar(annual); document.getElementById('cashOnCash').textContent = pct(cashReturn); document.getElementById('capRate').textContent = pct(capRate2);
formulas: Cash Flow = Rent − Expenses − Mortgage
Cash-on-Cash Return = Annual Cash Flow ÷ Down Payment
Cap Rate = NOI ÷ Purchase Price (ignores financing)
NOI = Annual Rent − Annual Operating Expenses

faq.question: What is a good ROI on rental property?
faq.answer: Cash-on-cash return of 8-12% is considered good. Cap rate of 5-10% is healthy. Markets with lower cap rates (expensive cities) often have better appreciation. Rural areas have higher cap rates but less appreciation.

faq.question: What expenses should I include?
faq.answer: Property taxes, insurance, maintenance (budget 1% of value/year), vacancy (5-8%), property management (8-10% of rent), HOA if applicable, and capital expenditure reserves (roof, HVAC, appliances).

faq.question: What is the 1% rule?
faq.answer: A property should rent for at least 1% of purchase price monthly. A $200K property should rent for $2,000+/month. It's a quick screening tool — many good investments don't meet it in expensive markets.

tagline: Compare cost of living between cities
keywords: cost of living calculator, cost of living comparison, city comparison, salary comparison
calcFn: var equivalent = currentSalary * (newIndex / currentIndex); var diff = equivalent - currentSalary; var pctDiff = ((newIndex - currentIndex) / currentIndex) * 100; document.getElementById('equivalentSalary').textContent = dollar(equivalent); document.getElementById('difference').textContent = (diff >= 0 ? '+' : '') + dollar(diff) + '/year'; document.getElementById('pctChange').textContent = (pctDiff >= 0 ? '+' : '') + pct(pctDiff, 1); document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = newIndex > currentIndex ? 'New city is more expensive. You need a raise to maintain your lifestyle.' : 'New city is cheaper! Your purchasing power increases.';
formulas: Equivalent Salary = Current Salary × (New Index ÷ Current Index)
Index 100 = national average
Common indices: NYC ~187, SF ~180, Houston ~96, Dallas ~101, Austin ~110

faq.question: How is cost of living calculated?
faq.answer: Cost of living indices weight categories: housing (30-40%), food (15%), transportation (10%), healthcare (10%), utilities (10%), and miscellaneous (15-25%). Housing is the biggest driver of differences between cities.

faq.question: What cities have the lowest cost of living?
faq.answer: Lowest major metros: Memphis, Oklahoma City, Birmingham, Little Rock, Wichita (indices 85-92). Highest: NYC (187), San Francisco (180), Honolulu (170), Boston (152). The gap is primarily driven by housing.

faq.question: Should I take a pay cut to move to a cheaper city?
faq.answer: If the cost-adjusted salary is higher, you're actually getting a raise. Moving from NYC ($100K) to Dallas ($70K) looks like a $30K cut, but Dallas costs ~46% less. $70K in Dallas = ~$102K NYC purchasing power — a net gain.

tagline: Compare salaries adjusted for cost of living
keywords: salary comparison calculator, salary cost of living, adjusted salary, salary equivalent
calcFn: var after1 = salary1 * (1 - stateTax1/100); var after2 = salary2 * (1 - stateTax2/100); var adj1 = after1 / (col1 / 100); var adj2 = after2 / (col2 / 100); document.getElementById('adj1').textContent = dollar(adj1) + ' (adjusted purchasing power)'; document.getElementById('adj2').textContent = dollar(adj2) + ' (adjusted purchasing power)'; document.getElementById('winner').textContent = adj1 > adj2 ? 'Offer 1 has ' + pct((adj1/adj2-1)*100, 1) + ' more purchasing power' : adj2 > adj1 ? 'Offer 2 has ' + pct((adj2/adj1-1)*100, 1) + ' more purchasing power' : 'Both offers are equivalent';
formulas: After-tax = Salary × (1 - State Tax Rate)
Adjusted = After-tax ÷ (COL Index ÷ 100)
Compare adjusted values for true purchasing power

faq.question: How do I compare salaries in different cities?
faq.answer: Divide each salary by the city's cost of living index, then compare. A $100K salary at COL 150 (SF) equals $66.7K in purchasing power. A $75K salary at COL 100 (average city) provides more actual buying power.

faq.question: Should I factor in state taxes?
faq.answer: Absolutely. States like Texas, Florida, and Washington have no income tax. California and New York charge 9-13%. This can mean $5,000-15,000+ difference in take-home pay on the same salary.

faq.question: What else should I consider besides salary?
faq.answer: Benefits value ($5K-30K), commute costs and time, career growth opportunities, quality of life, proximity to family, housing market trajectory, and the complete compensation package (equity, bonus, 401k match).

tagline: Calculate annual commuting costs by mode of transport
keywords: commute cost calculator, commuting cost, driving to work cost, commute expense
calcFn: var roundTrip = distanceMiles * 2; var weeksPerYear = 50; var daysPerYear = daysPerWeek * weeksPerYear; var fuelCostPerDay = (roundTrip / mpg) * gasPrice; var wearPerMile = 0.10; var wearPerDay = roundTrip * wearPerMile; var dailyTotal = fuelCostPerDay + wearPerDay; var monthlyTotal = dailyTotal * daysPerWeek * 4.33 + parkingMonth; var annualTotal = dailyTotal * daysPerYear + parkingMonth * 12; var commuteHoursPerYear = (roundTrip / 30) * daysPerYear; var hourly = annualTotal / commuteHoursPerYear; document.getElementById('dailyCost').textContent = dollar(dailyTotal); document.getElementById('monthlyCost').textContent = dollar(monthlyTotal); document.getElementById('annualCost').textContent = dollar(annualTotal); document.getElementById('hourlyEquiv').textContent = dollar(hourly) + '/hr commute time';
formulas: Fuel cost = (Round trip miles ÷ MPG) × Gas price
Wear & tear: ~$0.10/mile (tires, oil, depreciation)
Annual = Daily cost × Work days + Parking × 12
Effective hourly = Annual cost ÷ Hours spent commuting

faq.question: How much does commuting cost per year?
faq.answer: The average American spends $5,000-10,000/year commuting by car (15-mile one-way). This includes gas, wear and tear, insurance premium for mileage, and parking. Many people underestimate this because costs are spread across small daily amounts.

faq.question: Is it worth moving closer to work?
faq.answer: Often yes. Every mile closer saves ~$700/year in driving costs plus commute time. If you value your time at $25/hr, a 30-minute commute reduction saves $6,250/year in time value. Many people can afford $500-700 more/month in rent and break even.

faq.question: What is the true cost per mile of driving?
faq.answer: AAA estimates $0.65-0.75/mile for the average sedan including fuel, insurance, depreciation, maintenance, and financing. Gas alone is $0.12-0.18/mile. People who only count gas drastically underestimate their true driving cost.

tagline: Project population growth using exponential model
keywords: population growth calculator, population projection, growth rate calculator, demographic calculator
calcFn: var future = currentPop * Math.pow(1 + growthRate/100, years); var gain = future - currentPop; var doubleYears = growthRate > 0 ? 70 / growthRate : Infinity; document.getElementById('futurePop').textContent = fmt(future, 0); document.getElementById('popGain').textContent = '+' + fmt(gain, 0) + ' (' + pct(gain/currentPop*100, 1) + ')'; document.getElementById('doubleTime').textContent = growthRate > 0 ? fmt(doubleYears, 1) + ' years' : 'N/A (no growth)';
formulas: P(t) = P₀ × (1 + r)^t
Rule of 70: Doubling time ≈ 70 ÷ Growth Rate %
US average: ~0.5%/yr, World: ~0.9%/yr, Fast-growing cities: 2-3%

faq.question: What is the Rule of 70?
faq.answer: Divide 70 by the growth rate percentage to estimate doubling time. At 2% growth, population doubles in ~35 years. At 1%, ~70 years. This works for any exponential growth including money, populations, and infections.

faq.question: What US cities are growing fastest?
faq.answer: Fastest growing (2020-2024): Austin, Boise, Raleigh, Phoenix, and Nashville (2-4%/year). Declining: San Francisco, New York, Chicago, and Detroit. Population shifts are driven by jobs, housing costs, taxes, and climate.

faq.question: Is exponential growth realistic?
faq.answer: For short-term projections (10-20 years), exponential models work well. Long-term, growth slows as resources limit population (logistic growth). Most developed countries are approaching zero or negative natural growth rates.

tagline: Score retirement destinations on key factors
keywords: retirement location calculator, best places to retire, retirement score, retire where
calcFn: var weights = {col: 0.25, health: 0.25, weather: 0.20, tax: 0.15, safety: 0.15}; var weighted = colScore * weights.col + healthScore * weights.health + weatherScore * weights.weather + taxScore * weights.tax + safetyScore * weights.safety; var maxScore = 10; var pctScore = (weighted / maxScore) * 100; var grade = pctScore >= 80 ? 'A — Excellent' : pctScore >= 70 ? 'B — Good' : pctScore >= 60 ? 'C — Average' : pctScore >= 50 ? 'D — Below Average' : 'F — Poor Match'; var scores = [{name:'Cost of Living',v:colScore},{name:'Healthcare',v:healthScore},{name:'Weather',v:weatherScore},{name:'Taxes',v:taxScore},{name:'Safety',v:safetyScore}]; scores.sort(function(a,b){return b.v - a.v}); document.getElementById('totalScore').textContent = fmt(weighted, 1) + ' / 10 (' + fmt(pctScore, 0) + '%)'; document.getElementById('grade').textContent = grade; document.getElementById('strongest').textContent = scores[0].name + ' (' + scores[0].v + '/10)';
formulas: Weighted score: COL 25%, Healthcare 25%, Weather 20%, Taxes 15%, Safety 15%
Score 1-10 for each factor based on your research
80%+ = Excellent, 70-79% = Good, 60-69% = Average

faq.question: What are the best states to retire in?
faq.answer: Tax-friendly: Florida, Texas, Nevada, Wyoming (no income tax + no estate tax). Low cost: Alabama, Mississippi, Arkansas. Healthcare: Massachusetts, Minnesota. Weather: Florida, Arizona, Carolinas. It depends on your priorities.

faq.question: How much money do I need to retire?
faq.answer: The 4% rule: multiply annual expenses by 25. Spending $50K/year needs $1.25M. In a low-cost area ($35K/year), you need $875K. In NYC ($80K/year), you need $2M. Social Security provides $20K-40K/year for most retirees.

faq.question: Should I factor in state taxes for retirement?
faq.answer: Absolutely. 9 states have no income tax. 12 states don't tax Social Security. Some states exempt pension income. On $60K retirement income, moving from a high-tax to no-tax state saves $3,000-5,000/year.
